prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white female, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. Without the family’s actual essential monthly spending, use this formula:

## Recommended emergency fund: **9 months of essential expenses**

A one-income household with two young children needs a larger cushion than the standard 3–6 months. The mortgage-free home lowers monthly obligations, but it also creates unpredictable repair costs.

| Essential monthly spending | 9-month fund |
|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | $27,000 |
| $4,000 | $36,000 |
| $5,000 | $45,000 |
| $6,000 | $54,000 |

**Recommendation:** Target **$36,000–$45,000** if essentials are roughly $4,000–$5,000 per month. Keep this in an insured, liquid savings or money-market deposit account—not stocks.

Include groceries, utilities, property taxes, homeowners and health insurance, transportation, childcare, minimum debt payments, and basic medical costs. Exclude vacations, entertainment, extra debt payments, and retirement contributions. Because the home has no mortgage, also maintain a **separate home-repair reserve**—ideally around **1% of the home’s value annually**—so repairs do not consume the emergency fund.

Race does not change the calculation. Job stability, insurance deductibles, health needs, access to family support, and how quickly the unemployed spouse could return to work do.

This is educational guidance; a financial professional can tailor it to the household’s benefits and obligations.
